segments.s701-g25: زمانه is the subject meaning 'the age/times'. ز here is the poetic preposition 'because of' (not simple 'from'). شمشیر او is the agent phrase 'his sword' placed after ز. تیره گشت is a two-word predicate: تیره (an adjective 'dark/bleak') + گشت (past of گشتن 'became'), so together 'became dark/bleak because of his sword.'
segments.s701-g26: This clause follows a Subject‑Adverb‑Predicate order: سر نامداران ('the heads of the renowned') is the noun phrase subject, همه emphasizes 'all/everyone', and خیره گشت is the predicate where خیره (an adjective 'astonished') combines with گشت ('became') to mean 'became astonished' or 'were struck dumb.'
segments.s701-g27: نخستین سخن is a nominal phrase 'the first speech/words.' گویم is the first‑person present/subjunctive of گفتن used here as a performative 'I will say / I say.' از indicates the topic/target 'about' when followed by a personal name, so از اردوان = 'about Ardavan.'
segments.s701-g28: ازان is a demonstrative/old form 'of those / from those,' linking Ardavan to a class. نامداران روشن‌روان is a descriptive phrase 'renowned, bright‑souled (lit. clear‑soul) ones' that apposes or further describes Ardavan; روشن‌روان is a compound adjective applied to people.
segments.s701-g29: شنیدی is past/perfect 'have you heard' (a question expecting the listener's knowledge). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/what', and بر before the name here marks the target/recipient of the action 'upon/against' — so بر نامور سوفزای = 'upon the famed Sofzā' (i.e. what happened to him).
segments.s701-g30: چه آمد is a fixed interrogative/exclamatory pattern 'what came / what happened'. ز is causal 'because of / from', linking the following modifiers to the cause. پیروز ناپاک رای is a noun phrase 'victorious, impure counsel/plan' with پیروز 'victorious' qualifying رای and ناپاک 'impure/immoral' giving moral coloring to the counsel.
segments.s701-g31: رها کردن is the compound verb/infinitive 'to release/set free.' In epic condensed narrative the infinitive + کردن stands for the action 'they released' (subject omitted). ازبند پای قباد is a genitive/construct: ازبند 'from the bond/chain' + پای قباد 'Qobad's foot' — literally 'loosing Qobad's foot from the fetter.'
segments.s701-g32: وزان 'and from that/thereupon' links this clause to the previous action. مهتران here means 'the great/important ones' (an older superlative/collective sense). دادن is the simple verb 'to give' used transitively, او را is the definite object 'him', and بباد is a poetic result phrase 'to the wind' meaning 'to be ruined/abandoned' — together 'the great ones gave him up to ruin.'
segments.s701-g97: بدو گفت is a light-periphrastic phrase meaning “he said to him” (بدو = ‘to him’ + گفت = ‘said’). شاه‌ای خردمند مرد is a vocative apposition addressed to the addressee: شاه‌ای = “O king” (vocative marker -ای), خردمند modifies مرد, so خردمند مرد = “wise man.” Word order is typical Persian: indirect object + گفت, then a vocative phrase.
segments.s701-g98: برش گنج یابی ازین کارکرد is the reported clause following the speech; یابی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive/optative form (“you may/shall find” or older-present “you find”). برش here functions as a pronominal compound meaning roughly ‘for him/to him’ (archaic spelling) and links to گنج 'treasure' as its object; ازین = از + این “from this,” and کارکرد denotes the deed/action. The clause therefore states that by this action treasure will be found for him.
segments.s701-g99: مرو را گهر داد literally ‘(to) Merv (را = direct-object marker) he gave a jewel.’ Persian uses را to mark the definite/direct object. داد is the simple past ‘gave.’ The conjunction و links the two parallel verb phrases (‘gave a gem’ and ‘gave dinars’).
segments.s701-g100: گرانمایه یاقوت بسیار داد = ‘he gave many precious rubies.’ گرانمایه is an adjective ‘precious/costly’ modifying یاقوت ‘ruby.’ بسیار here means ‘many’/‘a great deal’ (quantity) and داد is again the past ‘gave.’ Word order is adjective–noun–quantifier–verb, common in Persian poetry.
segments.s701-g101: بدو گفت کاین نزد چوبینه بر begins another reported clause: بدو گفت = ‘he said to him.’ کاین is a demonstrative ‘that/this’ introducing the content. نزد here means ‘in the presence of / by’ (near someone), and چوبینه is a proper name or reference (here used as a locus/person), so نزد چوبینه = ‘by/at Chubīneh’s (presence). The final بر is a prepositional particle linking to the following clause (here functioning like ‘that’/‘as for’ or ‘to’ in older poetic usage).
segments.s701-g102: شنیده سخنها برو بر شمر contains a participial/imperative sequence: شنیده (past participle ‘having heard’ or adjectival ‘heard’) modifies سخنها ‘the words/speeches.’ برو is the imperative ‘go,’ and بر شمر = ‘to/against Shamir’ where بر functions as ‘to/toward’ and شمر is the proper name Shamr. Together the clause gives the instruction: ‘having heard the words, go to Shamr’ (or ‘go to Shamr who has heard the words’ depending on syntax).
segments.s701-g89: همه پاک شمشیرها برکشیم is a hortative clause: برکشیم is first‑person plural subjunctive/hortative (“let us draw/raise”). همه and پاک are prefixed modifiers that intensify شمشیرها — همه = “all/together” (here indicating entirety or everybody acting) and پاک works adverbially (“completely / cleanly”). The noun شمشیرها is the direct object (plural) that the verb governs.
segments.s701-g90: به جنگ اندورن رومیان را کشیم: به + noun marks movement/toward (“into the battle”); اندورن is an adverbial/locative modifier meaning “within.” رومیان را is the definite object marked by را, and کشیم is another hortative/subjunctive (“let us kill/slay” here). Word order places the goal (به جنگ اندورن) before the object and verb, a common option in classical Persian epic lines.
segments.s701-g91: چو خسرو ببیند سپاه تو را uses چو as a temporal subordinate conjunction “when.” ببیند is the present/subjunctive form of دیدن used here in a dependent temporal clause (“when he sees”). سپاه تو را is the direct object phrase (army + possessive + object marker را) of ببیند; word order places the object after the verb-forming clause but it is still its object.
segments.s701-g92: همان مردی و پایگاه تو را is a coordinated noun phrase giving what will be seen: همان modifies مردی (“that very/that same man”) and is followed by و linking پایگاه تو را (“your stronghold”), with تورا (تو + را) marking the proprietor + object. In this elliptical line the verb from the previous clause is understood to govern both coordinated objects.
segments.s701-g93: دلش زود بیکار ولرزان شود: دلش is “his heart” (noun + possessive clitic ش). زود is an adverb “soon/quickly,” and بیکار و لرزان are predicative adjectives joined by conjunctional sense (“idle and trembling”). شود is the intransitive copular/auxiliary “become,” so the clause means “his heart will soon become idle and trembling.”
segments.s701-g94: زپیشت چو روبه گریزان شود: چو is again temporal/subordinating “when.” زپیشت is a fused locative phrase meaning “from/in front of you” (ز + پیشت), giving the viewpoint; روبه here is the noun “fox” used metaphorically, and گریزان is a present participial/adjectival form “fleeing.” شود applies to this clause as well (“when he becomes like a fleeing fox before you”).
segments.s701-g95: بدان نامه‌ها مهر بنهاد شاه: بدان is a fused prepositional/demonstrative (به + آن) meaning “to/those,” here marking the target letters; نامه‌ها is the plural direct object “the letters.” مهر بنهاد is a verb phrase where مهر (seal) is the thing placed and بنهاد (past of نهادن) means “he set/placed.” شاه is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb phrase in poetic word order.
segments.s701-g96: ببرد ان پسندیده نیک‌خواه: ببرد is a past/optative form of بردن here “(he) carried/took” (or “he had taken”), ان is the demonstrative “that,” پسندیده is an adjective/noun “approved/pleasing (one),” and نیک‌خواه is a noun “well‑wisher / benevolent person.” Together the clause means he carried them to that approved well‑wisher.
segments.s701-g9: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 3rd‑person verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose or reported command ('that' / 'so that') and links forward to the verb نویسد; نامه‌های بزرگ is a noun phrase ('the great/important letters') that functions as the object of نویسد.
segments.s701-g10: نویسد is a present‑subjunctive / jussive form 'let him write' or 'he should write', here governed by تا (a commanded/desired action). بران is an archaic prepositional/demonstrative compound ('بر آن' ≈ 'to/for those') introducing the indirect object; مهتران is the plural noun 'elders/chiefs', and سترگ is a postpositive adjective meaning 'great/august', qualifying مهتران.
segments.s701-g11: بگستهم is a perfect/past verb 'he spread/arranged' (the object is implied). The sequence و ... و connects the following coordinated items (گردوی و بندوی) and the final گرد functions adverbially here ('around' / 'about' or 'made/placed about'), giving a colloquial/poetic word order.
segments.s701-g12: که introduces a subordinate (relative/result/purpose) clause. از مهتران = 'from/among the elders/chiefs' (از + plural noun). نام here means 'name/fame' (i.e. reputation), گردی is a troublesome form in the manuscript but functions syntactically with نام as part of a nominal phrase, and ببرد is a subjunctive/past form of بردن used here as 'may win/obtain' (purpose/result after که).
segments.s701-g13: چو ... چون is the comparative 'as/like' repeated for parallel examples: 'as Shapur and as Andiyan'. The noun سوار (postpositive here) qualifies اندیان, meaning 'mounted/horseman' or 'the rider', a typical poetic ordering where the epithet follows the name.
segments.s701-g14: هرآنکس 'whoever / any one who' begins a generalizing relative clause; که بود is the relative + past copula 'who was'; از یلان = 'from/of the heroes'; نامدار is a predicative adjective 'renowned/illustrious' describing that person.
segments.s701-g15: سرنامه is the nominative subject 'the heading/preface'. گفت here functions as the past verb 'said' (though the listed senses treat گفت as 'discourse', its use is verbal: 'it says/said'). از introduces the topic 'concerning/of', and جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'world‑Creator' (the Creator).
segments.s701-g16: همی‌خواهم is a present/frequentive construction 'I desire / I intend' (first person). اندر 'in/within' + نهان 'hidden, secretly' forms the adverbial phrase 'in secret', and آفرین here is a noun 'praise / acclamation' (i.e. 'to give praise'), so the clause expresses an intention to praise privately.
segments.s701-g1: چوآمد is a fused poetic form meaning 'when (it) came' (temporal clause head). به marks the indirect object/recipient ('to Bahram'). The sequence is topic/temporal (چوآمد) + indirect object (به بهرام) + the thing that arrived (زین آگهی).
segments.s701-g2: که introduces a reported clause 'that ...'. تازه شد is a compound where تازه (new/fresh) + شد (became/was) = 'was renewed/was made new'. آن فر شاهنشهی is the subject of شد: آن 'the/that', فر 'the (divine) royal glory', شاهنشهی 'of kingship/sovereignty'.
segments.s701-g3: همانگه is an adverbial 'at that moment/instantly'. ز لشکر is a poetic 'from the army' (ز = از). یکی نامجوی is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/one man, a name-seeker' (یکی = a certain; نامجوی = one who asks for a name or seeks to be named).
segments.s701-g4: نگه کرد is a light-verb compound where نگه here = 'gaze/look' and کرد = 'he did/made' → 'he looked/gazed'. با introduces accompanying manner 'with' (با دانش و آب روی = with learning and a fair/pleasing face). دانش = 'learning/knowledge'; آب + روی are literal elements ('water' + 'face') used poetically for a fair/clear countenance or reputation (here two separate nouns combined in sense).
segments.s701-g5: This is an identification clause: نام او بود 'his name was ...'. کجا is an archaic/interrogative marker here used to introduce the naming (literary usage), followed by the predicate داناپناه as the proper name/title (literally 'refuge of the wise').
segments.s701-g6: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. بهرام را uses را to mark Bahram as the beneficiary/target. او is the subject 'he'. The phrase بدی نیک‌خواه places بدی ('harm/evil') and نیک‌خواه ('well-wisher') together: poetically it means 'he was a well-wisher (نیک‌خواه) toward Bahram and not one who wished him harm' — the ordering is rhetorical rather than strictly syntactic.
segments.s701-g7: دبیر سرافراز را is the object phrase 'the honored (sara-farāz) secretary/scribe' marked by را. پیش خواند is a compound verb: پیش 'before/in the presence of' + خواند (here 'called/summoned') → 'he summoned forward'.
segments.s701-g8: سخنهای بایسته چندی is a noun phrase 'some/few fitting/necessary words' (سخنهای = speeches/words, بایسته = fitting/necessary, چندی = a few/some). براند is an archaic/poetic form from راندن used here in a causative/putting-forth sense 'to cause/bring forth; to utter/produce' — so 'he caused/ordered him to produce/utter some fitting words.'
segments.s701-g49: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning “when.” آیید is 2nd‑/3rd‑person plural present form of آمد (here: “come”) and heads the temporal clause. یکسر is an adverb “at once / all together” modifying آیید. به + نزدیک + من is a prepositional phrase meaning “near me” (به = toward/into, نزدیک = near, من = me). Word order is typical: temporal conjunction + verb phrase + adverbial phrase.
segments.s701-g50: شود is the 3rd‑person singular present/future of شدن meaning “become / will be,” introducing the result clause. روشن is a predicate adjective “bright/illuminated.” این جان تاریک من is the noun phrase object/subject of روشن (this + soul + adjective + possessor): “this dark soul of mine.” Persian places the verb before the nominal predicate in this poetic line; in English we render it as subject + verb.
segments.s701-g51: نیندیشم is a negated first‑person verb (“I do not think / I do not suppose”), used to introduce what the speaker finds unlikely. از روم is a prepositional phrase “from Rome” (i.e. from the Byzantines). وز here is the fused form of و + از (‘and from’) introducing the next source; شاهشان is the possessed noun “their king.” The whole segment expresses the speaker’s disbelief that help/submit will come from those sources.
segments.s701-g52: This clause states what the speaker deems unlikely. بپای اندر آرم is a verbal phrase with a goal/location phrase: بپای (به + پای) “to the foot(s)/at the feet,” اندر “into/at,” and آرم (1sg form) here functions as a placing/resting action (“I/they place/lay” in context — poetic variations of forms occur). سر و گاهشان is a coordinate NP “their head and their rank/time/position.” The literal sense is ‘(they) set/lay their head and status at (my) feet’ — i.e. submit. (Some lexical items are archaic/poetic and their forms may differ from modern uses.)
segments.s701-g53: نهادند is the simple past 3pl of نهادن “they placed / they set up.” برنامه‌ها is the plural direct object “plans / arrangements.” مهر اوی is a short phrase: مهر “favour / kindness” (here: favour, goodwill, or possibly a gift) and اوی is an enclitic referring to “his / for him.” Together: “they set up plans for his favour” or “they made arrangements on his behalf.”
segments.s701-g54: بیامد is the simple past “came.” فرستاده is the noun “messenger / one sent.” راه جوی is a compound of راه “road / way” and جوی “search” (here adjectival), literally “searching the road” — i.e. a courier/scout who seeks the way. Word order is verb + subject + modifier, common in poetry.
segments.s701-g55: بکردار is a prefixed adjectival/phrasing meaning “in the character/manner of” (lit. with the behaviour of); here it marks the guise in which the person traveled. بازارگانان is the plural “merchants,” the manner/guise head. برفت is the (poetic) past form meaning “he went” (classical ب/پ + رَفت), so the clause means “(he) went in the guise of merchants.”
segments.s701-g56: بدرگاه is the prepositional phrase “into the presence / to the court (of).” خسرو is the proper name “Khosrow.” خرامید is the past of خرامیدن “he strode / marched proudly.” تفت appears after خرامید as an adverbial/poetic intensifier in the line; the exact lexical entry in the provided senses does not match this poetic use, but the phrase as a whole reads “he strode in proudly / with proud bearing.”
segments.s701-g41: بساسانیان is a vocative/addressing form (بـ + ساسانیان) — 'O Sassanians'. تا introduces a temporal/subordinating clause meaning 'as long as' and takes the clause-ending verb ندارید ('you do not have') in the present; امید is the noun 'hope' as the object of ندارید. Word order here is vocative + subordinate temporal phrase ending with the verb.
segments.s701-g42: مجویید is a negative present/imperative form meaning 'do not seek' directed to plural 'you'; the direct object یاقوت ('ruby') follows. از introduces the source 'from/out of', with the adjective سرخ ('red') preceding the noun بید ('willow') in poetic order: 'from the red willow.'
segments.s701-g43: چواین is a compound of چو + این meaning 'when this'; نامه is the object 'letter'. آرند is a third-person plural present/subjunctive form of آوردن ('they bring') placed after the object (verb-final order typical of older/poetic Persian). نزد شما is a prepositional phrase 'to/near you.'
segments.s701-g44: که introduces a wish/optative clause here. فرخنده is the adjective 'auspicious/fortunate'; باد is the optative particle 'may' (not the noun 'wind') forming the wish 'may ... be'; اورمزد is the proper name 'Ahura Mazda' and شما is the addressee 'you.' Word order is somewhat inverted: 'that auspicious may be Ahura Mazda to you' → 'may Ahura Mazda grant you good fortune.'
segments.s701-g45: به نزدیک من is a prepositional phrase 'near me'; جایتان is the noun 'your place' with the possessive suffix -تان. روشنست is the predicative adjective plus copula (archaic/fused form) meaning 'is clear/bright.' The clause reads literally 'Near me, your place is clear.'
segments.s701-g46: برو is the imperative 'go'; آستی is an adverb 'slowly/gently' modifying the imperative. هم + ز + پیراهنست forms a verbless/elliptical nominal remark: هم 'also', ز 'from/of', پیراهنست here is a fused/poetic form meaning 'garmented / in a shirt' — so the sense is 'he/you also are clothed (in a shirt).' The subject of this short clause is implied; the construction is typical of poetic economy.
segments.s701-g47: بیک جای is an archaic poetic phrase meaning 'in one place' or 'at a single spot'; مان here functions as a first-person plural marker ('we/us') in older usage; بود is the past copula 'was/were'. آرام و خواب are predicates 'calm/peaceful and asleep'. The clause therefore reads 'We were in one place, calm and asleep.'
segments.s701-g48: اگر is used here as a temporal/conditional conjunction 'if/when'. تیره بد‌گر is a pair of epithets: تیره 'dark/ominous' modifies بدگر 'evil-doer/malignant one'. بلند is an adjective 'high' modifying آفتاب 'sun'. The verb is left elliptical (a common poetic device), yielding 'when the dark evil-doer lifted/raised the high sun.'
segments.s701-g65: This short clause places an accusative/reflective expression before the verb: چراخویشتن (lit. 'blaming oneself') is an adverbial/participial phrase; کرد is the past/preterite light verb 'did/caused'; باید is the necessity/modal 'must/ought'; and هلاک is the predicate noun 'ruin/doom'. Together they express a judgment — 'one who thus treats himself must be ruined.' Word order (object/phrase → verb → modal → predicate) is typical of older, poetic Persian.
segments.s701-g66: This is a descriptive clause: بلندی is used as a noun-like image 'a height/lofty thing' (here understood poetically), پدیدار is the noun/verb 'appearance/emergence', and گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/appeared' (verbal copula). ازمغاک is a prepositional phrase 'from the abyss' giving source. The clause means literally 'a loftiness/height appeared (became visible) from the abyss.'
segments.s701-g67: شوم here functions adjectivally 'ill‑omened' modifying نامه 'letter' (not as first‑person 'may I become'). نزدیک + خسروبرم is a locative/verb cluster: نزدیک 'near' plus خسروبرم, where برم is a first‑person verb form 'I bring/ I carry' attached to 'king' (classical phrase meaning 'to the king I bring'). The clause thus reads 'I brought an ill‑omened letter near the king.'
segments.s701-g68: The preposition phrase به نزدیک او 'to/into his presence' precedes the direct object هدیه 'gift'. نوبرم is a word for a new/choice offering (literally 'first‑fruit' / 'novelty'); here it is used as the thing the speaker bears. The verbal sense 'I bore/bring' is supplied by the context (the verb of the previous segment) so this short clause parallels the previous one: 'to his presence (I) bore a new gift.'
segments.s701-g69: باندیشه is an adverbial compound (با + اندیشه) meaning 'with thought' or 'with intent.' آمد is the simple past 'came' and the phrase به نزدیک شاه marks the destination/position 'to near the king.' The whole segment is straightforward: 'He came, intent, to the king's vicinity.'
segments.s701-g70: ابا is an archaic verb meaning 'to be reluctant / to refuse' and here governs a series of direct objects joined by و: هدیه و نامه و نیک‌خواه (gift and letter and well‑wishing). The last two words form the compound نیک‌خواه 'one who wishes well' or 'good‑wishing'; خواه is not operating as an auxiliary here but as part of that compound. The clause reads 'he refused the gift and the letter and (the) well‑wishing.'
segments.s701-g71: درم برد is a short noun+verb clause: درم (dirham, money) + برد (past of بردن 'took/transported') = 'he took money.' The conjunction و links to با نامه‌ها هدیه برد where با نامه‌ها 'with the letters' modifies هدیه برد 'bore/took gifts.' The verb برد repeats to apply to both objects in parallel structure.
segments.s701-g72: سخنهاش is the possessed plural 'his words' (سخن‌ها + -ش). برشاه here means 'before/upon the king' (بر + شاه) and گیتی qualifies that king as 'king of the world' (a title). شمرد is the past tense 'counted' or 'recounted/considered.' The clause thus says that he 'counted/rehearsed his words before the king of the world.'
segments.s701-g73: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign'. چون introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when/as'), نامه‌ها را is the definite direct object (را marks the object), and بخواند is a 3rd-person past narrative verb 'read'. Together they form 'When the sovereign read the letters.'
segments.s701-g74: مر is a dative/benefactive particle 'to me / before me' placed before the object for emphasis; او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks it); بکرسی زرین is a prepositional phrase 'on/at a golden throne' (ب- + کرسی = 'on the seat'); نشاند is an archaic past transitive 'set/caused to sit' (he seated/placed). Word order here places the dative ('مر') before the object for emphasis: 'he seated him before me on a golden throne.'
segments.s701-g75: بدو 'to him/unto him' is an indirect-object clitic-like element placed before the verb; گفت is the past verb 'said' (here the sense list did not include the verbal sense and is therefore marked below as unknown); کای is the vocative 'O', and مرد بسیاردان is the address 'man very-learned' (noun + postpositive adjective). The whole segment is the reporting clause 'He said to him: "O, man of great learning".'
segments.s701-g76: This is the direct speech clause. تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you' (address in the quoted speech); بهرام را is the definite object 'Bahram' (را marks it); نزد من is a prepositional phrase 'in my presence / before me' (نزد = 'in the presence of'); خوار دان is a verbal phrase where خوار = 'despised/despicable' is the predicate adjective and دان here means 'consider' — an imperative/jussive sense 'consider X to be Y'. So: 'You consider Bahram despised before me.'
segments.s701-g77: کنون 'now' sets the time. ز آنچ کردی is a prepositional phrase 'from that which you did' (ز = 'from', آنچ = 'that which', کردی = 'you did'). رسیدی is 2nd-person past 'you reached/attained' and بکام is a predicative phrase 'to satisfaction / to your desire' (بـ- + کام). The clause reads 'Now, from what you have done, you have attained your desire.'
segments.s701-g78: فزون‌تر is a comparative/adverbial 'more / greater'; مجو is the negative imperative 'do not seek' (مجو = مَجو, jussive/imperative negative); اندرین کار is a locative-demonstrative phrase 'in this affair' (اندرین = 'in this', کار = 'affair'); نام is the object 'name/reputation/fame'. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'Do not seek greater fame in this matter.'
segments.s701-g79: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb used for a ruler 'he commanded/ordered/said (politely)'. تا here introduces the resultant or purposive clause 'so that/and', and نزد او شد دبیر literally 'a secretary came to him' (نزد او = 'to/near him', شد = 'became/came to be', دبیر = 'secretary/scribe'). The clause expresses: 'He ordered it, so that a secretary came to him.'
segments.s701-g80: مران is a proper name (the scribe). پاسخ‌نامه را is the definite object 'the reply-letter' (را marks it). ناگزیر is an adjective 'compelled/forced'; here the verb is omitted by ellipsis (a common classical Persian construction), so it reads 'Maran was compelled (to produce/write) the reply-letter.'
segments.s701-g57: This noun phrase means 'a certain caravan carrying all sorts of things.' یکی is the indefinite demonstrative 'a certain,' followed by کاروانی (here used as a noun 'caravan/caravaneer'). ز + هرگونه چیز is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of/from every kind of thing' (z = poetic 'of/from'). Word order is determiner — noun — genitive phrase, as typical in Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s701-g58: This clause lists additional items the caravan carried: نامه‌ها (letters) and هدیه‌ها (gifts) with the past-tense verb داشت 'had' at the end. The enclitic نیز after the verb marks 'also/too.' The item ابا is lexically obscure here (an archaic/poetic form or variant reading); contextually the clause functions simply as 'it also had letters and gifts,' so I mark ابا as an unclear/archaic element.
segments.s701-g59: بدید is the simple past 'he saw' (poetic past). آن بزرگی و چنان سپاه is the compound object: آن بزرگی 'that greatness' and چنان/چندان سپاه 'so great a host' (چندان used as a quantifier/degree adverb modifying سپاه). The demonstrative آن makes the perception definite ('that/so great').
segments.s701-g60: که introduces a result or content clause 'that…'. گفتی here is parenthetical and functions like 'one would say' / 'you might say' (an impersonal or reported-speech use). مگر is rhetorical here (≈ 'isn't it?/indeed'), and the sequence بر زمین نیست راه literally places the predicate 'نیست' after the locative phrase 'بر زمین' to yield 'there is no way on the earth.' The clause therefore reads 'that (one) would say there is no road on the earth.'
segments.s701-g61: به دل گفت is the idiom 'he said to his heart,' i.e. 'he thought to himself.' The prepositional phrase (به دل) comes before the verb گفت (past 'said'), and با این‌چنین شهریار is the scene-setting prepositional phrase 'with such a king' that specifies the content of his thought.
segments.s701-g62: نخواهد is a negative modal/future-like form here meaning 'will not' / 'would not' (classical Persian uses such forms to express inability or non-occurrence). ز introduces the person referenced ('from/of'), so ز بهرام یل is an apposition 'of Bahram, the hero.' زینهار is an interjection 'beware!' or a warning appended to the clause. The whole segment expresses an inward warning: 'Bahram the hero will not (do it / prevail) — beware.' Note that the exact verb complement is left implicit in the epic style.
segments.s701-g63: This introduces a character: یکی مرد 'a certain man.' بی‌دشمنم is first‑person speech embedded in narration: بی-دشمن + ـم 'I have no enemy' (the speaker's own statement). پارسی is an epithet 'a Persian' attached to the man. In epic style, direct speech is often presented without quotation marks, so the first‑person form indicates the man's words.
segments.s701-g64: همان بار دارم is the man's declaration 'I have the same load' (همان = 'that same/just that'). دارم is present 'I have.' شتروار سی is a quantifying phrase: شتروار (literally 'in camel-fashion/caravan-fashion') plus the numeral سی 'thirty' — together indicating the amount 'thirty camel-loads' or 'a caravan of thirty camels.' The adjective/unit word شتروار is poetic/archaic and specifies the unit of load.
segments.s701-g81: نوشت is a past-tense reporting verb (classical/poetic usage) meaning 'wrote' where the plural ending is often dropped in epic style; اندران is a locative/adverbial 'in those / therein' that links the verb to the written medium; نامه‌های دراز is the noun phrase 'long letters' serving as the object/locative content. Together the phrase introduces reported content that will follow (hence the following 'که').
segments.s701-g82: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the reported clause; این مهتر is the demonstrative + noun 'this chief/leader'; گرد گردن‌فراز is an epithet where گردن‌فراز ('proud, haughty') is the adjective; the preceding گرد here functions as a rhythmic/poetic element attached to the epithet (classical style) rather than a separate preposition. The clause is therefore the beginning of the reported statement: 'that this chief, proud-necked...'.
segments.s701-g83: همه نامه‌های تو is 'all your letters' (possessive 'تو' follows the noun in classical Persian); برخواندیم is a past 1st-person-plural verb 'we read aloud / we recited' — the prefix بر- + خواند- emphasizes reading aloud or presenting the text. Word order is object phrase followed by the verb (typical).
segments.s701-g84: فرستاده را marks 'the messenger' as a definite direct object (را). پیش here means 'in the presence of / before (someone such as the king)'; بنشاندیم is past 1st-person-plural 'we seated' (transitive). The clause states the action done to the messenger after the letters were read.
segments.s701-g85: به گفتار بیکار is an adverbial phrase 'with/ in idle speech' (به + گفتار + adjective بیکار). با خسرویم is an archaic copular construction: خسرو (the king) + the suffix -یم yields 'we are (with) the king' — here it means 'we appear/are (in words) on the king's side.' The -یم ending functions as a 1st-person-plural copula in classical Persian.
segments.s701-g86: به دل 'in (the) heart' sets an inward, emotional frame; با تو 'with you' specifies the addressee in whose favor the speaker's feelings lie; همچون بهار 'like the spring' is a simile; نویم is again the archaic 1st-person-plural copular/verb-form ('we are') — together: 'in our hearts, with you, we are like the new spring.' Word order places the adverbial and prepositional phrases before the copula for poetic effect.
segments.s701-g87: چولشکر is an archaic/poetic contraction of چو لشکر 'when/as the army', introducing a temporal clause; بیاری here corresponds to به یاری 'with the help/companions of' (classical fused form) — 'with companions/aid'; بدین is the prepositional 'to/into this' (formed here as be- + this), and مرز وبوم is the compound 'frontier and homeland'. The whole segment means 'when, like an army and with companions, (they) came to this border and land.'
segments.s701-g88: که again introduces a subordinate clause; اندیشد is a past-tense verb 'he thought/was concerned' (classical 3rd-person form) and here carries the sense of being apprehensive or considering; از is the preposition 'of/from' marking the object of concern; گرز مردان روم is 'the mace(s) of the men of Rome' (گرز = mace, مردان = men/warriors, روم = Byzantium/the Romans). Together: 'that he feared/was thinking of the maces of the Roman men.'
segments.s701-g17: چوبیدار is a compound adjective/noun 'staff- or club-bearing' (i.e. armed with a staff). گردید is the past of گردیدن (classical use = 'became/turned' ~ 'rose up'); یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once'; ز + خواب = 'from sleep'. The phrase describes a sudden, complete waking-up while already armed.
segments.s701-g18: نگیرید here is an older/poetic perfective of the root گیر/گرفت meaning 'seized/took' (the initial n is not negation but part of the classical verbal form). بر بد = 'toward/against (the) bad/hostile' (بر = toward/against; بد = bad/foe), and ازین سان شتاب is a fixed phrase 'with such haste' (از + این + سان = 'in this manner/with such'). Together: 'he seized (it) and, with such haste, rushed against the foe.'
segments.s701-g19: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/so that'); تا here functions adverbially ('so that / in the extent that' or 'as far as'); درجهان is the fused spelling of در جهان 'in the world' (location for the following verb); تخم ساسانیان = 'the seed/offspring of the Sasanians' (تخم used metaphorically = origin/lineage).
segments.s701-g20: پدید آمد is a two-word verbal phrase 'appeared/was brought forth' (پدید 'manifest' + آمد 'came' = 'appeared'). اندر کنار و میان gives the locations/extent 'at the edge and in the middle' (اندر = 'in/within', کنار = 'beside/edge', میان = 'middle'), emphasizing that the seed/line showed up both at margins and at the centre.
segments.s701-g21: از ایشان = 'from them' (source phrase). نرفتست is an older perfective/impersonal past 'did not go forth / there did not issue' used idiomatically in the construction 'از X نرفتست جز Y' = 'nothing came from X except Y' (i.e. 'they had nothing but Y'). جزبرتری = 'except/other than superiority/preeminence.'
segments.s701-g22: This phrase lists the single things that did 'issue' from them. بگرد + گشتن is a classical verbal expression meaning 'to go about / to roam' (بگرد is the stem 'to turn/about' used with the infinitive گشتن 'to go'), جهان is the object 'the world', and داوری is a verbal noun 'judging / judgment'. Grammatically these are infinitival/verbal-noun items that complete the idea 'nothing came from them except: roaming the world and judging.'
segments.s701-g23: نخست = 'first/at first'. از سر بابکان is a genitive chain meaning 'from the head/chief of the Babakān' (از ... سر = 'from the leader of ...'); اردشیر names that leader and stands in apposition (i.e. 'Ardashir, the chief of the Babakān').
segments.s701-g24: که introduces a relative clause modifying اردشیر ('who'). اندر جهان = 'in the world' (location). تازه شد is a past construction 'became newly / newly came into being' and داروگیر is the predicate noun 'apothecary' (one who prepares or dispenses medicines). The clause therefore says that Ardashir newly appeared/rose up in the world as an apothecary.
segments.s701-g33: This is a short clause with the proper name subject first (قباد), followed by an adjectival/participial phrase بد اندیش 'ill‑thinking / ill‑intentioned' describing him. نیرو (object or complement) then appears before the perfective verb گرفت 'took/seized', a word‑order common in classical Persian where the verb follows its complements. گرفت is a past/action verb meaning 'took' or 'seized' here, so together the clause means 'Qobad, ill‑intentioned, took/gathered force(s)'.
segments.s701-g34: This clause continues the subject's actions: هنرها 'the arts/virtues' is the object (plural) and بشست is the past verb 'he washed (away)' — a literal 'wash away' used metaphorically to mean 'he discarded/cleansed off' (i.e. abandoned) those virtues. The following short phrase از دل آهو گرفت places a prepositional phrase (from the heart of the gazelle) before the verb گرفت 'seized', where از دل آهو functions as a source/ locus and گرفت again is the past verb 'seized/took'.
segments.s701-g35: چنان 'thus/so' is an adverb opening the sentence. The noun phrase نامور نیک‌دل را places two modifiers (نامور 'renowned', نیک‌دل 'kind‑hearted') before the object marker را, marking that phrase as the definite object. بکشت is an archaic/perfective verb form meaning 'slew/killed' (classical past of کشتن), so the clause reads 'thus he killed the famed kind‑hearted (one)'.
segments.s701-g36: This clause states the result: شد 'became' is the main verb (here a simple past/resultative), and دل نامداران 'the hearts of the renowned/notables' is the subject. درشت 'coarse/rough' is the predicative adjective. The initial برو is an adverbial/discourse particle (classical usage meaning roughly 'thereupon' or 'then') linking to the previous clause; its exact shade is archaic.
segments.s701-g37: کسی کو here functions as an indefinite/relative 'anyone/who', with کو being a classical relative particle. نشاید is an impersonal/presentive predicate 'it is not fitting' or 'one ought not'. The prepositional phrase به پیوند خویش 'for/to his own bond(s)' (پیوند = bond/tie) gives the domain or purpose — خویش (here split across خوی + ش) marks 'his own'. The clause thus sets up a prohibition: 'It is not fitting for anyone, with respect to his ties...'.
segments.s701-g38: هوا 'desire/whim' is the object of selection, while بر گزیند is a verbal phrase: گزیند is the present/optative form 'choose/select' and the preposition/particle بر here has the force 'upon/for' (classical construction 'هوا برگزیند' = 'choose from whim'). ز فرزند خویش 'from his children' is a source phrase (ز = 'from'), again with خویش marking possession. The combination after نشاید expresses 'ought not to choose out of whim among his children'.
segments.s701-g39: This is an impersonal negative: به بیگانگان 'to/for strangers' is the indirect object, هم 'also/even' adds emphasis, and نشاید again means 'it is not fitting'. بنیـز is an archaic/emphatic particle meaning roughly 'also/moreover' or reinforcing the negation — the whole clause says 'likewise it is not fitting (to give) to strangers'.
segments.s701-g40: نجوید کسی is a negative jussive-like clause 'let no one seek' (نجوید = 'seek/desire'). عاج 'ivory' is the thing sought; از چوب شیز 'from shiz‑wood' names the impossible source (ز/از = 'from', چوب = 'wood', شیز a type/kind with a sense of low value or obscurity). The line expresses a proverbial idea: one should not expect ivory from worthless wood.

sentences.s701-s13: The age darkened because of his sword; the heads of the renowned were all struck astonished.
sentences.s701-s14: Let my first words be about Ardavan — one of those renowned, bright‑souled men.
sentences.s701-s15: Have you heard what befell the famed Sofzā, from the victorious but impure counsel?
sentences.s701-s16: They loosed Qobad's foot from its bonds, and thereafter the great ones gave him up to the wind (i.e. abandoned/ruined him).
sentences.s701-s49: He said to him, “O king, wise man, you will find treasure for him through this deed.”
sentences.s701-s50: He gave a gem to Merv and gave dinars; he gave many costly rubies.
sentences.s701-s51: He said to him that this one was by Chubīneh; having heard the words, go to Shamir.
sentences.s701-s45: Let us all draw our swords cleanly; into the battle within let us slay the Romans.
sentences.s701-s46: When Khosrow sees your army, he will see that same man and your stronghold.
sentences.s701-s47: His heart will soon be idle and trembling, when like a fleeing fox he becomes turned from before you.
sentences.s701-s48: The king affixed a seal to those letters; he took them to that approved well‑wisher.
sentences.s701-s5: He commanded that great letters be written to those eminent chiefs.
sentences.s701-s6: He spread them out, and around (or: he placed) Gerdū and Bandū, so that he might win renown among the chiefs.
sentences.s701-s7: Like Shapur and like Andiyan the rider — whoever was one of the renowned heroes.
sentences.s701-s8: The preface said of the Creator of the world: 'I desire, in secret, to offer praise.'
sentences.s701-s1: When this news came to Bahram that the divine glory of kingship had been renewed.
sentences.s701-s2: At that moment he singled out from the army a certain man who sought a name, and looked upon him with learning and a fair countenance.
sentences.s701-s3: His name was Danāpanaah, the refuge of the wise; he was a well-wisher of Bahram and wished him no harm.
sentences.s701-s4: He summoned the honored scribe before him and bade him to utter a few fitting words.
sentences.s701-s25: When you all come straight up close to me, this dark soul of mine will become illuminated.
sentences.s701-s26: I do not imagine that from Rome and from their king they will (humbly) lay down their heads and their rank at my feet.
sentences.s701-s27: They put plans in place for his favour; a messenger, seeking the road, came.
sentences.s701-s28: Disguised in the manner of merchants he went to Khosrow’s court; he strode in proudly.
sentences.s701-s21: O Sassanians, as long as you have no hope, do not seek rubies from the red willow.
sentences.s701-s22: When they bring this letter to you, may Ahura Mazda grant you good fortune.
sentences.s701-s23: Your place near me is bright and clear; go slowly — he too is clothed in a shirt.
sentences.s701-s24: We stayed in one place, calm and asleep, when the dark evil-doer raised the high sun.
sentences.s701-s33: Why should one bring ruin on oneself? A towering height rose up from the abyss.
sentences.s701-s34: I came near the king with an ill‑omened letter; near him I bore a fresh gift.
sentences.s701-s35: He came with intent to the king; he refused the gift and the letter and (the) well‑wishing.
sentences.s701-s36: He took dirhams, and with the letters he bore gifts; he counted his words before the king of the world.
sentences.s701-s37: When the sovereign read the letters, he seated him before me on a golden throne.
sentences.s701-s38: He said to him, 'O man of great learning, consider Bahram despicable in my presence.'
sentences.s701-s39: Now, from what you have done you have attained your desire; seek not greater fame in this matter.
sentences.s701-s40: He commanded it, and a secretary came to him; Maran was compelled to (produce) the reply-letter.
sentences.s701-s29: A certain caravan, carrying all kinds of things, also had letters and gifts.
sentences.s701-s30: He saw that greatness and so vast a host that one might say there was no road left on the earth.
sentences.s701-s31: He said to his heart: 'With a king like this, Bahram the hero will not (prevail) — take heed!'
sentences.s701-s32: A certain man — a Persian who said 'I have no enemy' — said: 'I have that same load, thirty camel‑loads.'
sentences.s701-s41: They wrote in those long letters that this chief, proud-necked, ...
sentences.s701-s42: We read all your letters aloud, and we seated the messenger before (the king).
sentences.s701-s43: In idle words we are with the king; in heart, with you, we are like a new spring.
sentences.s701-s44: When, like an army, (they) with companions came to this frontier and land, for he feared the maces of the Roman men.
sentences.s701-s9: He rose wholly from sleep with his club in hand; he seized (it) and, with such haste, rushed toward the attack.
sentences.s701-s10: so that, in the world, the seed (line) of the Sasanians appeared, arising at the edge and in the midst (of affairs).
sentences.s701-s11: From them there issued nothing but preeminence: roaming the world and adjudication (i.e. their chief traits were world-roaming and judgment).
sentences.s701-s12: First (was) Ardashir, chief of the Babakān, who newly appeared in the world as an apothecary.
sentences.s701-s17: Ill‑intentioned Qobad gathered his forces; he washed away his virtues and seized the gazelle's heart.
sentences.s701-s18: Thus he slew the famed, kind‑hearted man; thereafter the hearts of the notables grew coarse.
sentences.s701-s19: No one ought, for the sake of his own bonds, to choose by whim from among his children.
sentences.s701-s20: Nor is it fitting to give (it) to strangers either; let no one seek ivory from shiz‑wood.
